title: Building a CAPA System That Closes the Loop
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How to build a CAPA system that closes the loop: risk-based triage, defensible root cause analysis and effectiveness checks for UK and EU pharma quality teams.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner showing a gowned pharmaceutical quality professional at a brushed stainless-steel workstation, calmly tracing a closed-loop quality workflow across a translucent glass board and a tablet, shot at a slight three-quarter angle with shallow depth of field. The palette is dominated by cool teal and cyan tones layered over deep navy shadows, with a single subtle green accent glowing from a process status indicator. Soft natural light falls from a frosted window, catching metal edges and a blurred cleanroom corridor in the far background, while the board and tablet stay in crisp focus and the surroundings fall gently out of focus, conveying precision, accountability and continuous improvement. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a correction, a corrective action and a preventive action?
faqs.a: A correction is the immediate fix to the specific problem found, such as quarantining an affected batch or re-issuing a faulty document. A corrective action removes the root cause so the same problem cannot recur, while a preventive action tackles that vulnerability anywhere else it could arise before it does. Inspectors expect all three to be handled and evidenced separately rather than collapsed into a single quick fix such as re-training one operator.

faqs.q: How do effectiveness checks make a CAPA system more inspection-ready?
faqs.a: An effectiveness check is pre-defined evidence, gathered after a sensible interval, that a corrective action actually worked and the problem has not returned. Implementing an action is not the same as proving it was effective, and missing or token effectiveness checks are among the most frequently cited CAPA weaknesses in EU and MHRA inspections. If a check fails, the correct response is to reopen the CAPA and revisit the root cause rather than force a closure to hit a metric.

faqs.q: How does ICH Q9 risk management help manage CAPA workload?
faqs.a: ICH Q9 quality risk management lets you scale investigation effort to the actual risk, which stops a CAPA system from drowning in low-value records. A minor, well-understood, one-off event with no product impact may need only a correction and a brief justification, whereas a recurring data-integrity or contamination issue warrants a deep, formally documented investigation. This risk-based triage, combined with trending to spot recurrence, keeps attention on the issues that genuinely threaten product quality.
